facts: The patient's name is Kyle.
The patient has a cough.
The cough was diagnosed as bronchitis by the patient's primary care physician.
The patient had a chest x-ray.
The chest x-ray found a lung nodule.
The patient had a CAT scan.
The CAT scan confirmed the lung nodule.
The patient was referred to the doctor.
The patient is nervous.
The patient has horses.
The patient loads hay for the horses.
The patient started coughing more after loading hay.
The patient got a cold.
The patient exercises.
The patient does a lot of biking.
The patient typically doesn't get sick.
The patient's cough has gone away at times.
The patient has a history of rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient takes methotrexate.
Methotrexate is taken for rheumatoid arthritis.
The patient takes 7.5 milligrams of methotrexate.
Methotrexate is taken once a week.
The patient's rheumatoid arthritis is fairly controlled.
The patient's joints feel okay when active.
The patient has problems with their hands.
The patient hasn't had recent rheumatoid arthritis flares.
The patient used to smoke.
The patient stopped smoking about 7 years ago.
The patient feels better after stopping smoking.
The patient has no pain.
The patient has no shortness of breath.
The patient's mother had breast cancer.
The patient's mother is doing well now.
The patient has no history of lung cancer.
The patient has no history of lung infections or pneumonia.
The doctor performed a physical exam.
The patient's vital signs are good.
The patient's blood pressure is 124/76.
The patient's heart rate is 70.
The patient's respiration rate is 16.
The patient's oxygen saturation is 98% on room air.
The patient is afebrile.
The patient's lungs are clear and equal bilaterally.
The patient's heart has a regular rate and rhythm.
The patient has no clicks, rubs, or murmurs in their heart.
The patient has no extra beats.
The patient has swelling on their knuckles.
The patient has intact pulses in all extremities.
The patient has brisk capillary refill.
The patient had a chest CT before seeing the doctor.
The chest CT showed a solitary 2-centimeter nodule in the right upper lobe.
The nodule is smooth in appearance.
There is no evidence of emphysematous disease.
The doctor ordered a pulmonary function test.
The pulmonary function test is to check the patient's lung function.
The doctor scheduled a PET CT.
The PET CT is to determine if the nodule is metabolically active.
The doctor suggested removing the nodule via video-assisted thoracoscopy.
The procedure will involve three small incisions on the side of the patient's right chest.
The patient will be under general anesthesia.
The procedure will take about an hour and a half.
The patient will have a chest tube after the procedure.
The chest tube will be removed the following day.
The patient will be in the hospital for one night.
The patient will go home the next day.
The doctor wants the patient to continue following up with their rheumatologist.
The doctor wants the patient to continue their medication therapy as previously outlined.
The patient is scared.
The doctor will be with the patient throughout the treatment process.
